rxn_class_summary: Nickel-catalyzed electrochemical 1,2-diarylation of 1,3-dienes with aryl iodides under mild conditions without chemical reductants
electrochemical_role: Cathodic reduction regenerates low-valent Ni(I) species from Ni(III) (and Ni(II)) intermediates in the Ni(I)/Ni(III) catalytic cycle, replacing the need for a stoichiometric chemical reductant

key_intermediates.name: Ni(I)X-Ln (species A, active catalyst generated in situ from Ni(II))
Ar-Ni(I)-Ln (species C, after cathodic reduction of B)
allyl/homoallyl Ar-substituted Ni(I) species D (after migratory insertion of 1,3-diene into Ar-Ni bond)
Ar-Ni(III)(Ar')-Ln species E (second oxidative addition with aryl iodide)

electron_flow: Ni(II) precatalyst is cathodically reduced to Ni(I) (A); A undergoes oxidative addition with ArI to give Ni(III) (B), which is cathodically reduced to Ar-Ni(I) (C); migratory insertion of the 1,3-diene gives allyl-Ni(I) (D), a second oxidative addition with Ar'I gives Ni(III) intermediate E, and reductive elimination delivers the 1,2-diarylated product while regenerating Ni(I) for the next cycle.

probe_reagents.name: chiral PyOx/BiOx ligands (L1-L7) for asymmetric control
probe_reagents.observation: Up to 56% ee for diarylation (L6); ligand L7 switches chemoselectivity from diarylation to hydroarylation (5a, 35%, 1% ee), implicating divergent Ni-allyl reactivity

probe_reagents.name: solvent (DMA) as putative H-atom source in hydroarylation
probe_reagents.observation: Authors state the hydrogen of hydroarylation product may originate from the solvent (no explicit D-labeling reported on this page)

notes: Mechanism is proposed based on literature precedent and ligand-controlled chemoselectivity (diarylation vs hydroarylation) rather than direct mechanistic probes on these pages. Authors explicitly do not rule out an alternative Ni(0)/Ni(II) pathway (cycle through A', B', C') shown in Scheme 5. Regioselectivity is exclusively 1,2- (>20:1 vs 1,4-) and E/Z >20:1 for diarylation products.
